The creek changed color for about two days. Muddy brown, like something upstream churned up the bottom.
Tracks that size do not come from anything I recognize. Too wide for a wolf, too narrow for a bear.
The ridge shifted after the last heavy rain. Not much, but enough that the old trail does not line up with the pass anymore.
Fish stopped appearing in the lower pool. The upper stretch still has them, so something is blocking the run between the two.
A fox den appeared under the granary foundation. The vixen moved her kits in overnight and the farmers are divided on whether to chase them out or leave them be.
Bats left the eastern caves all at once. A whole colony, gone overnight. They usually roost there year-round.
There is a patch of ground near the old foundation where nothing grows. Bare soil in a circle, maybe ten paces across.
The hawks are nesting lower than usual this season. The falconer says that means the prey has moved down from the heights.
A fallen tree blocked the lower trail for a week before anyone cleared it. The wood underneath had gone soft and white, like it rotted in days instead of months.
The rock face on the north side is weeping moisture where it never did before. Something underground must have shifted.
A landslide blocked the ravine after the tremor. Small one, but enough to dam the stream and create a new pool.
The wildflowers bloomed a full month early. Everything is out of sequence and the pollinators have not caught up.
An old growth stump near the ruins is sprouting new shoots. Whatever killed the original must have finally leached out of the soil.
The owls moved out of the bell tower after nesting there for years. Something drove them off, but the tower looks the same as always.
The lichen on the bridge pilings has turned a deep rust color. The stoneworker says it means the water chemistry changed, but she cannot say why.
The moss on the standing stones grows on the wrong side. It should face the moisture but it faces the ridge instead.
There is a sinkhole forming in the meadow south of here. Slow, but the edges are crumbling inward a little more each week.

The soil near the old forge site has a metalite discoloration that extends about twenty paces in every direction. Nothing planted there tastes right. The herbalist says the groundwater carries the taint.
A pair of herons moved into the upper pond this season. They have never nested here before, which the birdwatcher says means the fish population has reached a threshold worth their attention.
The water table has risen noticeably in the last two years. Cellars that were dry for a decade are now seeping. Something changed upstream and nobody can say what.
The oak blight spread from the western stand to the trees along the road. The forester says it follows the prevailing wind. If that is true, the orchard is next.
An entire hillside of ferns turned brown in the space of a week. Not seasonal. Something in the soil shifted. The root systems were intact but the fronds looked scorched.
The wolf pack that used to den near the northern ridge has not been seen in two seasons. Either they moved on or something displaced them. I would like to know which.
A spring that had been dry for years started flowing again after the tremor. Clean water, good volume. The engineers are debating whether to build an intake or leave it alone in case it stops again.

The birch trees along the creek are showing stress bands in the bark. Horizontal cracks, evenly spaced. The forester says it indicates repeated freeze-thaw cycles, but the pattern is more regular than weather alone would produce.

A beaver dam appeared on the lower creek without anyone noticing the beavers. The dam is well-built, which means they have been here for weeks at minimum. The farmers downstream are not happy about the changed water flow.
The fungal growth on the fallen logs in the eastern wood has changed color from white to a vivid orange. The herbalist collected samples and says she has not seen the species before.
Deer are coming closer to the settlement than they used to. The sentries see them at the tree line every morning. Either they are less afraid or they are being pushed from somewhere else.

The root system of the old cedar has cracked the foundation stones of the watchtower. The mason says the tree has to come down. The elders say the tree was here first. The foundation, meanwhile, continues to crack.

A colony of ground-nesting wasps established itself on the footpath to the lower well. The path has been rerouted but the detour adds ten minutes. Nobody wants to disturb the nest because the last person who tried was stung forty times.
The river carved a new channel during the spring flood and left the old crossing point on dry land. The ford stones are sitting in a field now, twenty paces from the nearest water.
A stand of young pines died simultaneously despite being healthy the previous season. The forester says the soil pH changed, likely from runoff. He blames the tannery. The tanner says prove it.

The migratory birds arrived three weeks late this year. When they finally came, there were half the usual numbers. The birdwatcher has been keeping records for fifteen years and says this is the lowest count on record.

There is a thermal vent in the limestone outcrop that keeps a small area warm enough for plants to grow year-round. Ferns, mosses, even some flowering species in the dead of winter. The herbalist guards it jealously.

The erosion along the riverbank has exposed layers of clay, gravel, and what appears to be an older riverbed about six feet below the current one. The geologist who passed through said the river used to flow twenty paces east of where it runs now.

A massive spiderweb complex appeared in the abandoned barn overnight. Not one web: dozens, interconnected, spanning the entire interior. The silk is unusually thick and the spiders themselves are larger than anything local.

The groundwater near the mine entrance has turned slightly acidic. The plants within fifty paces are yellowing at the edges. The miners say it is natural drainage. The farmers downstream say it was not happening before the mining started.
A tree that was struck by lightning last season is still standing but the bark has peeled off in a perfect spiral from crown to root. The exposed wood is bone white and has not weathered at all.

The algae bloom in the upper pond turned the water a dense green. Fish cannot breathe in it and the downstream creek smells of rot. The herbalist says it will clear when the temperature drops, but the damage to the fish stock is already done.

An underground stream surfaced in the basement of the old mill. The flow is steady, about a bucket per minute, and the water is ice cold. The miller sees it as a problem. The brewmaster sees it as an opportunity.

The songbirds disappeared from the eastern wood for about a week. No explanation. Then they came back, all at once, as if nothing had happened. The silence during that week was more disturbing than any storm.

A geode the size of a head was exposed by erosion on the cliff trail. The interior crystals catch the morning light and throw colored reflections across the rock face. It is stunning, and I give it a month before someone pries it loose.
The elk rut started two weeks early this year. The bulls are aggressive and territorial well outside their normal range. The sentries have had to reroute two patrols to avoid confrontations.

A patch of bioluminescent fungus appeared in the root cellar of the tavern. Faint blue-green glow, visible only in total darkness. The tavern owner sealed the cellar. The herbalist wants access. The negotiation is ongoing.

The river otters have been fishing in the millpond, which was never part of their range before. Their presence means the natural pools are depleted. The fishermen confirm this: catches are down by a third.

A rockslide on the eastern cliff exposed a seam of mineral that catches the light. Blue-green, possibly copper ore. The smith is interested but the cliff face is unstable and nobody wants to mine it until the loose stone is cleared.

The grape vines on the southern slope are producing fruit a full size larger than last year. The vintner credits the unusually wet spring. The soil composition has not changed, so the moisture must be the variable.

A dead zone appeared in the lake where no fish or plants survive. Roughly circular, maybe thirty paces across. The water inside it looks the same as everywhere else but nothing lives in it. The fishermen mark it with a buoy and steer around it.
The pine resin production has dropped sharply this season. The trees are not damaged, they are just not producing. The forester thinks it is a stress response but cannot identify the stressor.

An ant colony of unusual size has established itself near the grain store. The mound is waist-high and the colony extends underground in every direction. The exterminator says he has never seen one this large and is reluctant to disturb it.

The soil in the cleared field has a layer of charcoal about a handspan below the surface. Continuous, uniform, covering the entire field. Something burned there a long time ago: thoroughly, evenly, and completely.

A vine species nobody can identify has been growing on the old stone wall. Aggressive, fast-growing, and the tendrils seem to find every crack in the mortar. The mason wants it removed. The herbalist wants to study it first.

The salmon run was the largest anyone can remember. The creek was so dense with fish you could nearly walk across on their backs. The bears know this and have been seen closer to the settlement than is comfortable.

A section of forest floor has subsided by about a foot, creating a shallow depression that fills with water after rain. The depression follows a straight line for about a hundred paces, as if something beneath the surface collapsed along a corridor.

The dragonflies appeared in numbers three times the normal population this year. Swarms of them over the pond every evening. The mosquito population dropped to nearly zero within a week. Nature's pest control is more efficient than ours.

The hillside terracing that the first settlers built is eroding. The retaining walls were dry-stacked stone and the soil behind them is washing through the gaps. Rebuilding them properly would take a full crew for a season.

A tree fell in the upper wood and when it hit the ground the impact exposed a burrow system that extends in multiple directions. Not a single animal den: a network. Whatever lives in there has been building for years.
The river stones downstream of the hot spring are coated in a mineral deposit that forms in thin, colorful layers. Red, yellow, white, in bands. The layers grow about a finger's width per year.

The clay deposit on the south bank has a vein of pure white kaolin running through it. The potter says it fires to a porcelain-like finish and is the best material she has ever worked with. She is being territorial about it, which I understand.

A raptor species nobody can identify has been hunting in the fields. Larger than a hawk, darker coloring, and a call that sounds like tearing fabric. The falconer wants to trap one for study. The farmers just want it to keep eating the rodents.

The aquifer that feeds the main well is recharged by snowmelt from the upper ridge. In a low-snow year, the well drops by midsummer. This year the snowpack was below average and the well is already showing signs.

The morel mushrooms appeared in the burned clearing exactly as the forester predicted. She says they fruit in the year following a burn, something about the ash chemistry triggering the mycelium. She mapped every burn site and harvests them on a rotation.

A massive boulder in the streambed has shifted noticeably downstream over the last five years. The water has undercut the foundation and the current does the rest. It moves about a handspan per season. Slow, but when it reaches the bridge pilings it will be a problem.

The swallows returned to the eaves of the main hall for the seventh consecutive year. They build new nests each time, directly on top of the old ones. The stack is getting impressive. The mason says the weight will eventually damage the fascia.

The canopy coverage in the old-growth section has become so dense that the understory is dying from lack of light. The forest floor is bare except for fungi and shade-tolerant mosses. It feels like walking through a building with a living roof.

A seep at the base of the limestone bluff has deposited enough calcium to form a small terrace of travertine. The formations are delicate and growing: thin curtains of stone, mineral pools with rims. The water is drinkable and faintly sweet.

The meadow grass in the lower field grows taller and greener in a stripe about three paces wide that runs the entire length of the field. There must be something buried beneath it: a pipe, a channel, or a decomposing structure that feeds the soil.

The woodpeckers have been working the same dead elm for two months. The drumming is constant and audible from the road. The tree is hollowed out to the point where it should have fallen by now, but it stands. I would not walk under it.

A stand of aspens on the hillside share a single root system. When one tree is stressed, the others show symptoms. The forester says they are technically one organism. The oldest root mass could be centuries old.

The fireflies have not appeared this season. In previous years the meadow was bright with them every evening from late spring. The herbalist suspects the new pesticide the farmers are using. The farmers say it targets different insects. The fireflies, either way, are gone.

A limestone cave near the upper trail has formations that drip a mineral-rich water. The stalactites grow imperceptibly but the drip rate is measurable: about one drop every eight seconds. The cave floor beneath them has built up cone-shaped stalagmites that are perfectly centered. Geological patience.

The invasive thornbush that was imported as a hedge plant has spread well beyond the original planting. It now covers several acres of former pasture and the thorns are long enough to puncture boot leather. Removing it costs more than planting it did, which is the usual story with invasives.

The underground river that the miners intersected surfaces about a quarter mile downstream as a spring. The water carries dissolved minerals that stain the rocks orange where it emerges. The discoloration has been spreading, which means the flow rate has increased.

A family of marmots has taken up residence in the rubble pile behind the storehouse. They whistle when anyone approaches, which the sentries appreciate as an inadvertent early warning system. The quartermaster is less grateful because they have been into the root vegetables.

The treeline has moved downhill by at least a hundred paces in the last decade. Seedlings have established where there used to be open meadow. The shepherds complain that it reduces grazing area. The forester says the forest is reclaiming what was originally its territory.

A fossil bed exposed by the rivercut contains shell imprints in solid stone. Sea creatures, here, hundreds of miles from any coast. The geologist says this was all underwater once. It is difficult to look at these mountains the same way after learning that.

The wild boar population has exploded since the wolves left. Without the predators, the boar root up the forest floor and destroy the understory. The balance that the wolves maintained is gone and nothing has replaced it.

A spring seep has created a mineral lick that draws every herbivore within a day's travel. Deer, elk, goats, rabbits: all converging on a patch of mud the size of a room. The hunters know about it. The animals know the hunters know about it. Neither side has adjusted strategy.

The ancient oaks along the ridge have a canopy so wide that nothing grows beneath them for twenty paces in every direction. The ground is a carpet of dry leaves and acorns. The absence of competing growth is the tree's own doing: the root system takes everything.

A section of the riverbed has shifted from gravel to sand over the last three seasons. The current slowed, the heavier particles settled, and the sand migrated in. The ford is deeper now and the bottom is unstable. The crossing is still usable but not safely.

The lichens on the standing stones are growing at a measurable rate: about a finger's width per year. The oldest colonies, if the rate has been constant, suggest the stones have been standing for at least four hundred years. The lichens are a better record than any written history we have.

A new hot spring appeared in the lower valley after the last tremor. Small, barely warm enough to notice, but the mineral content is high enough to leave a white crust on every surface the water touches. The herbalist is testing the water for medicinal properties.

The cliff swallow colony on the bridge has grown from a dozen nests to over a hundred in three years. The mud nests are packed so tightly they have merged into a solid mass. The bridge keeper says the weight is not a concern. The structural engineer who visited said otherwise.

The brambles in the cleared field have root runners that extend underground for twenty paces in every direction from the visible plant. Cutting the surface growth does nothing. You have to excavate the entire root network or it regenerates within a season.

The spring peepers started calling two weeks before the last frost. Their timing is usually reliable enough to plant by. This year they were wrong and several farmers who trusted the frogs lost their early seedlings.

A stone outcrop near the trail has a natural arch formed by water erosion. The arch is maybe eight feet tall and four feet wide. It frames the view of the valley so perfectly that visitors assume it was carved deliberately. It was not.

The predator-prey cycle in the local ecosystem has shifted. Rodent populations are up, raptors have followed, and the smaller songbirds are being displaced. The birdwatcher says it will rebalance within two seasons. The farmers say the rodent damage cannot wait two seasons.

The peat bog on the eastern lowland has a surface that bounces when you walk on it. The mat of vegetation floats on water and the whole thing undulates like a living floor. It is disorienting and the footing is treacherous if you do not know the solid paths.

A mineral vein in the cliff face oxidizes in bands of color: rust red, malachite green, sulfur yellow. After rain, the runoff paints the rock below in streaks of color that last until the next dry spell. The stoneworker calls it nature's stained glass.

The old-growth trees near the ravine have root systems so massive they have lifted and cracked the bedrock. The roots follow fracture lines and widen them over decades. The trees are literally breaking the mountain apart, one crack at a time.

The hedgehog population has collapsed in the lower fields. The herbalist blames the loss of the insect base. The farmers blame the hedgehogs for digging in the beds. Either way, the slugs that the hedgehogs used to eat are now unchecked and the gardens are suffering.

A colony of burrowing owls has moved into the old quarry. The holes in the spoil heap suit them perfectly and the hunting ground around the quarry is open enough for their low-altitude flight. The quarry has not been this productive in years, though not in the way anyone intended.

The runoff from the meadow carries enough organic matter to feed an algae bloom every spring. The bloom feeds the tadpoles, the tadpoles feed the frogs, and the frogs fill the night with a chorus so loud it drowns out conversation. Nobody minds. It means the system is healthy.

A section of the bluff has been hollowed out by wind erosion into a series of shallow alcoves. Each one is distinct: different depth, different shape, different acoustic signature. Whistle into one and the sound resonates. Whistle into the next and it falls flat.

The crayfish in the upper creek have grown to an unusual size. Claws as long as a finger. The fishermen say the population is small but the individuals are enormous, which suggests they have outlived their competitors.

The blackberry thickets along the old fence line produce fruit every other year. In the off years, the energy goes into root expansion. The pattern is so reliable that the children know which years to bring baskets and which years to stay home.

A patch of moss in the cave entrance glows faintly under certain conditions. Not bioluminescence: the structure of the moss reflects trace amounts of light from the cave opening. In total darkness it is invisible. In dim light, it marks the path.

The meadow voles have dug a tunnel network beneath the snow that becomes visible only when the spring melt reveals the trails. A web of packed-earth channels, some extending hundreds of paces. An entire winter's architecture, exposed for a week, then overgrown.

A solitary pine at the summit has grown almost horizontal from decades of prevailing wind. The trunk extends six feet before turning upward. The bark on the windward side is smooth and polished. The bark on the lee side is rough and thick. The tree is a compass.

The creek pools below the waterfall harbor a population of fish that grow no larger than a finger. The pool is isolated: too high for upstream migration, too steep for downstream. The fish have been evolving in this closed system for as long as the waterfall has existed.

The soil near the old charcoal site supports only one species of grass: a coarse, blue-green variety that tolerates the residual alkalinity. Everything else dies. The grass has formed a perfect circle that maps the original burn area.

The osprey pair that nested on the dead snag over the lake have returned for the fifth year. They rebuild the nest each spring, adding to the previous year's structure. The nest is now large enough to be visible from the settlement. The fishermen consider them good luck.

A layer of volcanic ash buried beneath three feet of soil suggests an eruption that predates any oral history. The forester found it while digging drainage. The ash is pale grey, fine-grained, and compresses like powder. Whatever produced it was far away but powerful.

The hawthorn grove blooms earlier on the south-facing edge than on the north. The difference is about ten days. Standing in the middle, you can see both: one side in full flower, the other still budding. The bees work the early side first and the late side keeps them fed longer.

The kingfisher that hunts the millpond dives from the same branch every time. The branch is worn smooth where it perches. When the branch finally broke, the bird spent two days testing alternatives before settling on a new one. The fishermen noticed the disruption before they noticed the broken branch.

An old beaver dam that had been abandoned for years is now being recolonized. Not by beavers: by a community of insects, amphibians, and water plants that have turned the stagnant pool into a functioning wetland. The dead wood provides structure. The decay provides nutrients. The system built itself.

The frost line has risen by several feet over the past decade. Root cellars that used to stay frozen through spring are now thawing by late winter. The change is subtle year to year but cumulative over a generation. Food preservation depends on reliable cold. The cold is no longer reliable.

The fern beds on the forest floor follow the light gaps with a precision that looks planned. Where a tree fell and opened the canopy, the ferns fill the space exactly. The boundary between fern and bare ground tracks the boundary between light and shade. Nature wastes nothing.

A stand of willows along the creek has developed root structures so dense they have slowed the current to a near standstill. The pool behind them is silting up. In another decade it will be a meadow. The willows will have created the conditions for their own displacement.

The hawk moth caterpillars stripped the foliage from every nightshade plant in the garden within a week. The herbalist says the population spike is cyclical: every seven or eight years, they overwhelm their food source and then crash. The plants recover. The caterpillars do not.

The sandbar at the river bend has shifted position three times in two years. Each flood deposits sediment differently and the bar migrates downstream. The ferry operator has to adjust the landing point after every high water event.

A pair of ravens has nested on the watchtower for the last four years. The guard feeds them scraps and they have learned to recognize her. When her shift changes, the ravens follow the new guard for an hour before returning to the tower. They are particular about their people.

The mushroom species that grows on the rotting bridge timbers is one the herbalist has been trying to cultivate for years without success. It grows only on wood that has been submerged in running water for a specific period. The bridge provides the exact conditions by accident.

A windthrow exposed the root ball of an oak that had stood for at least two centuries. The root mass is taller than a person and the soil clinging to it contains layers of history: charcoal fragments, pottery shards, and a seam of gravel from a stream that no longer exists.

The cliff nesting site has been used by peregrines for as long as anyone can remember. The ledge is stained white from generations of droppings. The birds return to within inches of the same spot each year. The falconer says the site has likely been in continuous use for centuries.

A mudflat exposed at low water contains tracks that fossilized in the clay: three-toed prints from something that has not walked here in a very long time. The prints are detailed enough to show skin texture. The geologist measured them at nearly two feet across.

The elder berry bushes along the fence line fruited so heavily this year that the branches split under the weight. The herbalist harvested what she could and the rest fell and fermented on the ground. The wasps discovered the fermented fruit and the fence line became impassable for a week.

A natural spring in the rock face flows through a crack barely wider than a finger. The water pressure is enough to project a stream two paces from the cliff. The pressure has been constant for as long as anyone has measured it, which suggests a reservoir that nobody has located.

The moth migration passed through the settlement at night and the morning revealed thousands of them resting on every vertical surface. Wings folded, perfectly still, covering the walls in a layer of brown and grey. By noon they had moved on and the walls were bare.

The clay banks of the lower river contain a layer of blue clay that the potter says is the finest natural material within a hundred miles. The layer is only accessible at low water and only in a section about fifty paces long. She guards the location as if it were a vein of gold.

A grove of birch trees on the north slope has bark so white it is visible from the ridge across the valley. At dawn the light catches them and they glow. The forester says the whiteness indicates a specific mineral content in the soil that the birches concentrate in the bark.

The creek produces a natural sound that changes pitch with the water level. At high water it is a low roar. At low water it is a ringing, almost musical note. The children call it the singing creek and the name has stuck even among the adults.

A limestone pavement near the upper trail has dissolved into a pattern of deep grooves called clints and grykes. Each groove is a miniature ecosystem: ferns, mosses, and small plants growing in the protected channels while the flat surfaces are barren and wind-scoured.

The wren population in the hedgerows has doubled in two years. The birdwatcher attributes it to the decline in the sparrowhawk that used to prey on them. Without the predator, the wrens are expanding into habitat they previously avoided. Balance, when disrupted, produces surprises.

An erosion gully that started as a minor wash three years ago has widened to six feet and deepened to four. The soil is loose and the slope steep enough that every rainstorm carries more material. The farmer whose field borders it says it will reach his fence by next season.

The marsh reeds at the edge of the pond have grown so thick that the open water is contracting. Ten years ago the pond was visible from the road. Now it is hidden behind a wall of reeds. The ducks approve. The fishermen do not.

A colony of swifts occupies the gaps in the stone wall every summer. Their arrival date is consistent to within three days each year. The departure date has been getting earlier, which the birdwatcher says indicates a shift in the insect emergence pattern that the swifts depend on.

The fallen oak in the clearing has become a nurse log: seedlings growing from its decomposing trunk in a straight line. Birch, maple, and fern, all rooted in the rotting wood. The dead tree is more productive than it was alive, in its way.

A seam of iron-rich stone in the creek bed stains the water orange for about two hundred paces downstream. The staining is permanent in the sediment and temporary in the water. Visitors think the creek is polluted. The forester explains it is geological and always has been.

The badger sett on the hillside has been occupied for so long that the entrance mounds are large enough to sit on. The badgers have excavated a network that the forester estimates extends thirty paces in every direction. The soil they have moved over the decades is measured in tons.

A pocket meadow in the forest has a microclimate warm enough to support species that do not grow anywhere else in the region. The herbalist found three plants there that she had only seen in dried specimens from distant climates. The meadow is sheltered from every wind by the surrounding canopy.

The toad migration crosses the main road every spring. For about a week, the road is covered with them at dusk. The carter refuses to drive after dark during that period. The toads do not care about the inconvenience they cause.

A dead elm has become the most ecologically productive tree in the area. Woodpeckers, nuthatches, and owls nest in the trunk. Beetles colonize the bark. Fungi decompose the heartwood. The living trees around it host half as many species. Death, it turns out, is habitat.

The river meander has been migrating slowly toward the settlement for years. The outside bank erodes while the inside bank deposits. The surveyor says the river has moved twelve paces in a decade. At that rate, the closest building has about forty years before the bank reaches its foundation.

The elderflower season lasted only ten days this year instead of the usual three weeks. The herbalist says the timing is tied to rainfall in the preceding month. She harvested what she could but the syrup production is a third of normal.

A landslip on the northern trail exposed a cross-section of soil that reads like a history book. Topsoil, clay, gravel, sand, and a dark band of organic material that the forester says was a forest floor, buried by centuries of accumulation.

The mayflies hatched in such numbers that the air above the river was thick with them. They lived for a single day, mated, and died. By morning the riverbank was carpeted with their bodies. The fish fed for a week on the surplus.

A pair of foxes has denned within sight of the chicken coop. The farmer expected the worst but the foxes have been hunting rabbits in the field and ignoring the chickens entirely. The farmer does not trust the arrangement and the foxes do not care.

The clay soil in the western field cracks in a hexagonal pattern during dry spells. Each hexagon is roughly the same size. The forester says it is a property of the clay shrinkage, but the regularity is uncanny. It looks like a floor laid by a careful mason.

The snails in the garden have reached a population density that the gardener describes as biblical. They consume a row of seedlings overnight. The ducks that used to control them were eaten by the fox that no longer lives in the area. One absence cascades.

A hollow oak near the crossroads has been adopted by a swarm of bees. The honey they produce has a distinctive flavor that the herbalist says comes from the oak tannins in the heartwood. She has been trading it at a premium and the tree's value now exceeds any timber calculation.

The creek water is clearest in early spring before the algae grow and clearest again in late autumn after the first frost kills them. For about two weeks in each period, you can see the bottom at its deepest point. The rest of the year, you are guessing.

A stone wall that was built a century ago has been colonized by a succession of plants that now form a complete ecosystem. Mosses at the base, ferns in the crevices, ivy on the face, and a small tree growing from the capstone. The wall is more alive than built.

The mushroom fairy ring in the upper meadow has been expanding by about a pace per year. The ring is now over thirty paces across. The forester says the mycelium network beneath it is a single organism and one of the largest living things in the area.

A colony of sand martins has excavated a bank along the river into a honeycomb of nesting holes. The bank face looks like it has been peppered with projectiles. Each hole is precisely the same diameter. The colony returns every year and adds more.

The wild garlic that grows along the shaded bank produces a smell so strong in spring that you can detect it from the road. The forester says the intensity correlates with soil moisture. The wetter the spring, the stronger the garlic. This year the smell reached the settlement.

The oak galls on the trees along the lane are larger than usual this season. The herbalist collects them for ink making. She says the size indicates a heavy infestation of gall wasps, which means the tree is under stress. Good for ink. Bad for oaks.

A flood deposited a layer of fine silt across the lower field that was so uniform it looked like someone had smoothed it with a trowel. The silt is mineral-rich and the crops that season were the best in a decade. The flood was a disaster that produced a bounty.

The crickets have gone silent in the western field while the eastern field is as loud as ever. The dividing line follows the road. The herbalist suspects a soil treatment that the western farmer applied is killing the cricket larvae. The farmer denies using anything unusual.

A woodpecker has been storing acorns in the holes of the gatehouse timber. Hundreds of acorns, pressed into every available crevice. The gatekeeper considers it damage. The birdwatcher considers it architecture. The acorns, meanwhile, are sprouting.

The river otter that hunts the stretch below the bridge has become so accustomed to people that it fishes in plain view. The fishermen consider it competition. The children consider it entertainment. The otter considers them irrelevant.

A fallen tree across the trail created a natural dam that backed up a small pond. Frogs colonized it within a week. Dragonflies within two. A heron within a month. The pond is less than a year old and already has a complete food web.

The thistle population in the north pasture has reached the point where the livestock refuse to graze there. The thistles are native and ecologically valuable but economically devastating. The farmer wants them gone. The herbalist wants them studied. The thistles want nothing and continue to spread.

A limestone slab near the upper trail has hundreds of tiny fossil shells embedded in its surface. The rain has etched away the surrounding stone and left the shells standing in low relief. You can run your fingers across them and feel individual ridges. The slab is a museum that nobody curated.

The chestnut trees along the avenue are showing signs of the blight that devastated the groves two valleys over. The forester identified the early symptoms and is treating the most valuable specimens. Whether treatment will work is unknown. The disease has a perfect record elsewhere.

A vernal pool that forms each spring in the depression behind the storehouse supports a population of fairy shrimp that exist nowhere else within walking distance. They hatch, grow, breed, and die in the six weeks the pool persists. When the pool dries, their eggs wait in the dust until the next spring.

The morning mist on the lake creates optical effects that the locals call ghost ships. Light refraction bends the far shore upward and inverts it, producing the illusion of structures floating above the water. Visitors are startled. Residents barely notice anymore.

The elm bark beetles have finished off the last mature elm in the area. The tree was the largest single organism in the settlement's territory. The foresters could not save it and the lumber is contaminated with beetle galleries. Even in death, it belongs to the beetles.

A series of pools carved into the limestone by seasonal waterfalls form a natural staircase. Each pool is deeper and colder than the one above it. The bottom pool is deep enough to swim in and cold enough to make you regret it. The children swim there every summer regardless.

The dormice in the hedgerow are so numerous this year that you can hear them rustling from the road. The population boom follows a mast year for the hazels: heavy nut production means more food means more dormice. Next year, when the hazels rest, the dormice will crash.

A migrating eel was found in the upper well, which has no connection to any surface water. The well is fed by an aquifer. The eel must have traveled through underground channels to arrive there. The fishermen say this is impossible. The eel says otherwise.

The ivy on the south wall of the old barn has grown thick enough to support its own weight. The tendrils have worked into the mortar joints and are now structural: the ivy holds the wall together as much as the wall supports the ivy. Removing it would collapse the corner.

A lightning-struck tree produced a Lichtenberg figure burned into the bark: a branching, fern-like pattern that traces the path the electricity took from crown to root. The pattern is as detailed as any drawing and was produced in an instant. The tree survived.

The granite erratic that sits in the middle of the lower field was deposited by glacial action long before anyone lived here. It is taller than a person and the nearest matching granite is fifty miles north. The stone traveled here under ice and has been sitting in the field ever since.

The pond has developed a thermocline in summer: warm water on top, cold water on the bottom, with a sharp transition between them. You can float in warm water and dangle your feet in water ten degrees colder. The fish stay in the cold layer and the swimmers stay in the warm one.

A pair of storks built a nest on the chimney of the meetinghouse and the council has been using the back entrance to avoid disturbing them. The storks are considered good luck and the council is not in a position to test that belief.

The nettle patch behind the storehouse grows taller than a person by midsummer. The herbalist says nettles indicate nitrogen-rich soil, which means something organic decomposed there in quantity. Nobody knows what was buried, but the nettles are grateful for it.

A spring flood deposited a massive tree trunk on the bank: a species the forester does not recognize from the local watershed. It must have come from far upstream, traveling for miles in the high water. The wood is dense, dark, and harder than any local timber.

The gentian flowers on the upper meadow bloom in such concentration that the hillside turns blue for two weeks each year. The color is visible from the settlement. Travelers who arrive during the bloom say it is the most striking natural feature they have seen.

A section of the old stone wall has been undercut by tree roots so gradually that the wall appears to float above the ground on a cradle of wood. The roots bear the weight of the masonry and the masonry shelters the roots from weather. Neither planned it, but the partnership works.
